anchor.snippet: A natural number is divisible by 3 if the sum of its digits in base 10 is also divisible by 3.

annotations.label: Reverse/permutation divisible by 3

anchor.snippet: the reverse of any number that is divisible by three (or indeed, any permutation of its digits) is also divisible by three

annotations.note: Mathlib has the divisibility-by-3 ↔ digit-sum criterion, from which permutation invariance follows, but no statement specifically about reversed/permuted digits.

annotations.label: Divisibility rule generalizes to bases ≡ 1 (mod 3)

anchor.snippet: This divisibility rule works in any positional numeral system whose base divided by three leaves a remainder of one

annotations.note: `Nat.dvd_iff_dvd_digits_sum b b' (h : b' % b = 1)` gives `b ∣ n ↔ b ∣ (digits b' n).sum` for any base `b'` with `b' ≡ 1 (mod b)`.

annotations.label: 3 is the second smallest prime, first odd prime

anchor.snippet: 3 is the second smallest prime number and the first odd prime number.

annotations.note: Mathlib has `Nat.prime_three` but no statement ranking it as the second prime or first odd prime.

annotations.label: 3 is twin prime with 5, cousin prime with 7

anchor.snippet: It is a twin prime with 5 , and a cousin prime with 7

annotations.note: Mathlib does not define twin primes or cousin primes.

annotations.label: Triangle is smallest non-self-intersecting polygon

anchor.snippet: It is the smallest non-self-intersecting polygon and the only polygon not to have proper diagonals

annotations.note: Mathlib has `Polygon` and `Affine.Triangle` but no theorem that 3 is the minimum vertex count for non-self-intersecting polygons.

annotations.label: 3 is the first Mersenne prime
annotations.note: Mathlib defines `mersenne` (with `mersenne 2 = 3`) and primality tests, but does not state that 3 is the smallest Mersenne prime.

annotations.label: 3 is the first of five known Fermat primes

anchor.snippet: It is also the first of five known Fermat primes

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `Nat.fermatNumber` with `fermatNumber_zero : fermatNumber 0 = 3`, but does not enumerate the known Fermat primes.

annotations.label: 3 is the second Fibonacci/Lucas/Sophie Germain/factorial prime

anchor.snippet: It is the second Fibonacci prime (and the second Lucas prime ), the second Sophie Germain prime , and the second factorial prime

annotations.note: Mathlib has no definitions of Sophie Germain primes, Fibonacci primes, Lucas primes, or factorial primes.

annotations.label: 3 is the second and only prime triangular number

anchor.snippet: 3 is the second and only prime triangular number

annotations.note: Mathlib has no definition of triangular numbers as such and no statement classifying prime triangular numbers.

annotations.label: Gauss's three-triangular-number theorem

anchor.snippet: Carl Friedrich Gauss proved that every integer is the sum of at most three triangular numbers

annotations.note: Mathlib does not contain Gauss's three-triangular-number theorem.

annotations.label: 3 is the only prime one less than a perfect square

anchor.snippet: Three is the only prime which is one less than a perfect square

annotations.note: No direct statement found that 3 is the unique prime of the form n²−1.

annotations.label: Integers modulo 3 form the smallest finite field of odd characteristic

anchor.snippet: The integers modulo 3 form the finite field

annotations.note: `Mathlib/Algebra/Field/ZMod.lean` provides `instance : Field (ZMod p)` for prime `p`, but minimality among odd-characteristic finite fields is not separately stated.

annotations.label: Elliptic curves in characteristics 2 and 3

anchor.snippet: elliptic curves in characteristics 2 and 3 do not always admit short Weierstrass equations

annotations.note: Mathlib defines general `WeierstrassCurve` over any commutative ring but does not isolate the characteristic-2/3 obstruction to a short form.

annotations.label: Three non-collinear points determine a plane and a circle

anchor.snippet: Three non-collinear points determine a unique plane in a three dimensional affine space, and a unique circle in a Euclidean plane.

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `circumcenter`/`circumsphere` of an affinely independent simplex (including triangles), giving the unique circle through three non-collinear points, but the plane-determination statement is not packaged as a named lemma.

annotations.label: Rotational symmetry of order 3

anchor.snippet: An object has rotational symmetry of order 3 if it is unchanged by a rotation of one third of a full turn, or 120 degrees.

annotations.note: Mathlib has rotation maps but no general definition of an object's rotational symmetry of order n.

annotations.label: Triangle as a polygon with three edges and vertices

anchor.snippet: The triangle , a polygon with three edges and three vertices , is the most stable physical shape.

annotations.note: `Affine.Triangle k P := Simplex k P 2` defines a triangle as three affinely independent points.

annotations.label: Pythagorean characterization of the triad

anchor.snippet: the number 3, which they called triad , is the only number to equal the sum of all the terms below it, and the only number whose sum with those below equals the product of them and itself

annotations.note: This Pythagorean numerological characterization of 3 is not formalized in Mathlib.
